DHB1243 Cake Making · Chapter 1

identify cake making preparation requirements.

Before a single tin goes in the oven: the history, the ingredients, the cake families and the methods behind every great bake.

Start Chapter 1 Watch baking tutorials
1.1 · The story so far

A short history of cake making

The word "cake" is older than the fluffy, frosted thing we picture today. For most of history a cake was closer to enriched bread: ancient Egyptian bakers sweetened dough with honey, and medieval European "cakes" were dense, fruited loaves leavened only by yeast and the air beaten in by hand.

The cake we recognise now is largely a story of better ingredients and better tools. Refined white sugar and finely milled flour became affordable, bakers learned to whip eggs into a stable foam for sponge cakes, and in the 19th century the arrival of chemical leaveners — baking soda and then baking powder — meant a light crumb no longer depended on hours of beating or a tin of yeast.

The final leap was heat control. Once ovens could hold a steady, measurable temperature, recipes could be written as repeatable formulas rather than guesswork. Everything in this chapter — weighing, mixing methods, timing, testing for doneness — grows out of that shift from craft-by-feel to craft-by-method.

1.2 · Why each one is there

Ingredient functions

A cake is a balancing act between ingredients that build structure and ingredients that tenderise it. Understanding the job each one does is what lets you fix a recipe instead of just following it.

🌾

Flour

Provides structure. Its gluten and starch set in the heat to form the crumb that holds everything up.

🧁

Sugar

Sweetens, but also tenderises, holds moisture, and helps the crust brown and caramelise.

🧈

Fat (butter / oil)

Adds richness and moisture, shortens gluten strands for a tender crumb, and traps air when creamed.

🥚

Eggs

Build structure and bind the batter, add richness, and — when whipped — provide lift through trapped air.

🎈

Leavening

Baking powder, soda or whipped air create gas bubbles that expand in the oven to raise the cake.

💧

Liquid (milk / water)

Hydrates the flour, dissolves the sugar and activates leaveners, controlling the batter's consistency.

🧂

Salt

Sharpens and balances flavour and strengthens gluten slightly so the structure holds.

🌰

Flavourings

Vanilla, cocoa, zest and extracts give the cake its identity without changing the core balance.

1.3 · Two big families

Types of cakes

Almost every cake belongs to one of two families, defined by what gives it lift and richness: high-fat cakes built around butter, and egg-foam cakes built around whipped eggs.

1.3.1High-fat / butter / shortened cakes

These rely on a generous amount of fat for their tender, moist, close crumb. Air beaten into the fat (or chemical leaveners) provides the rise. They are rich and keep well.

  • Pound cakes ‚— the classic example, traditionally equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs and flour.

1.3.2Sponge / egg-foam cakes

These get their light, airy structure from eggs whipped to a foam rather than from fat. They are lighter and springier, and are the base for many layered and rolled cakes.

  • Genoise ‚— a whole-egg foam sponge, the workhorse of European layer cakes.
  • Swiss rolls ‚— thin, flexible sponges baked to be rolled around a filling.
  • Chiffon cake ‚— a hybrid that adds oil and whipped whites for a moist yet airy crumb.
  • Angel food cake ‚— made from whipped egg whites only, with no fat at all.
1.4 · How the batter comes together

Mixing methods

The mixing method is what turns the same handful of ingredients into very different cakes. It decides how air gets in and how much structure develops. Methods split along the same two families as the cakes themselves.

1.4.1High-fat cake methods

Creaming method Two-stage (high-ratio) method One-stage (liquid shortening) method Combination creaming / sponge method

In the creaming method, fat and sugar are beaten together first to trap air, then eggs and dry ingredients follow. The two-stage and one-stage methods are faster, machine-friendly approaches that combine ingredients in fewer additions, while the combination method borrows from sponge technique for extra lightness.

1.4.2Egg-foam cake methods

Plain sponge / Genoise Butter sponge / Butter Genoise Separated-egg sponge Biscuit / Jaconde Chiffon Angel food

These all build structure from whipped eggs. Some use whole eggs (Genoise), some separate the whites and yolks to whip each (separated-egg, biscuit), and some — chiffon and angel food — depend almost entirely on a stable meringue of whipped whites for their lift.

1.5 · Getting ready to bake

Preparation of cake making

Five preparation requirements, in order

1.5.1

Selection & preparation of pans

Choose the correct size and shape for the recipe, then grease and line it. Proper pan prep stops sticking and helps the cake bake and release evenly.

before mixinggrease & line
1.5.2

Scaling cake batters

Weigh the batter into each tin so layers bake to the same height and finish at the same time. Accurate scaling is the difference between even, professional layers and lopsided ones.

by weighteven layers
1.5.3

Temperature & timing

Preheat fully and bake at the temperature the recipe specifies. Too hot sets the crust before the centre rises; too cool and the cake dries out before it sets.

preheatfollow the recipe
1.5.4

Test for doneness

A cake is done when it springs back to a light touch, has shrunk slightly from the sides, and a skewer in the centre comes out clean.

checkclean skewer
1.5.5

Cooling

Rest briefly in the tin, then turn out onto a rack to cool completely before filling or frosting. Cooling sets the crumb and stops trapped steam making it soggy.

rackfully cold
1.6 · Judging the result

Evaluate the quality of cakes

A good cake is judged against a few consistent criteria. Learning to read these tells you not just whether a cake is good, but which part of the process to adjust next time.

  • Volume & shape ‚— well risen and even, with a level or gently domed top.
  • Crumb & texture ‚— an even, fine crumb appropriate to the cake type, neither dense nor full of large holes.
  • Colour ‚— an even golden crust with no pale or burnt patches.
  • Moisture ‚— moist and tender, not dry or gummy.
  • Flavour & aroma ‚— clean, balanced and true to the recipe.
1.7 · When it goes wrong

Faults & causes

Most cake faults trace back to a specific mistake in mixing, measuring or baking. This table pairs common problems with their usual causes.

FaultLikely cause
Sunken centreToo much leavener, underbaking, or opening the oven too early.
Dense, heavy crumbNot enough air creamed or whipped in, or over-mixing the flour.
Domed, cracked topOven too hot, or too much flour developing the gluten.
Dry, crumbly cakeOver-baking, too much flour, or too little fat or liquid.
Coarse, holey textureUnder-mixing, or too much leavener creating large bubbles.
Pale, soft crustOven too cool, or not baked long enough.
1.8 · The shortcuts that exist

Types of convenience product

Alongside making everything from scratch, the trade uses convenience products to save time and guarantee consistency. They sit on a spectrum from "just add water" to "ready to serve."

  • Complete cake mixes ‚— pre-blended dry ingredients that need only liquid, fat or eggs added.
  • Concentrates & bases ‚— partial blends a baker finishes with their own ingredients for more control.
  • Pre-made fillings & frostings ‚— ready-to-use creams, jams and icings.
  • Frozen & par-baked products ‚— cakes or layers ready to thaw, finish or assemble.
1.9 · What's moving now

Trends & product innovation

Cake making keeps evolving with taste and technology. Knowing current trends helps a baker stay relevant and spot opportunities for new products.

  • Health-conscious bakes ‚— reduced sugar, wholegrain, gluten-free and plant-based recipes.
  • Premium & artisan flavours ‚— single-origin chocolate, real fruit, and unusual flavour pairings.
  • Visual design ‚— minimalist finishes, bold colour, and dramatic cross-sections for social media.
  • Personalisation ‚— custom, small-batch and made-to-order cakes for individual occasions.
1.10 · The final step

Display & packed product

How a cake is presented and packed protects it and sells it. Good display keeps the product fresh and appealing right up to the moment it's bought or served.

  • Attractive display ‚— clean stands, good lighting and clear labelling that show the cake at its best.
  • Protective packaging ‚— sturdy boxes and supports that keep decoration intact in transit.
  • Hygiene & freshness ‚— food-safe materials and storage that maintain quality and shelf life.
  • Clear information ‚— ingredients, allergens and dates so the customer knows exactly what they're buying.
